answer:
For Allama Iqbal, *wataniyat*—translated here as territorial nationalism or simply "Nationalism"—is a modern idol forged by Western civilization and its philosophy. He portrays it as the greatest among "newer gods," one that desecrates Islamic faith by masquerading in religious garb while laying waste to the Prophet's teachings on unity and piety.[^1] It confines believers to a single geographic place (a "calamity"), sows rivalry among peoples, turns trade into conquest, corrupts politics with deceit, and severs the universal brotherhood of Islam.[^2]

Iqbal offers **Islam itself as the true "homeland"** (*vatan*) in its place—not a fixed territory of land defined by the state, but a divine, universal command encompassing the strength of God's Oneness (*tawhid*), service to the Prophet, and freedom to roam like a fish in the ocean.[^3] He urges Muslims ("O Mustafavi," i.e., followers of Muhammad) to reject nationalism's "hollow idol" through faithful action.[^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 "Wataniyat" (translation), [p-79b1e9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 "Wataniyat" (translation), [p-97937b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b) and [p-a723b2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 "Wataniyat" (translation), [p-2737d5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5) and [p-a723b2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 "Wataniyat" (translation), [p-2737d5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5).
